text:
Aussies have reacted online after breaker Rachael 'Raygun' Gunn busted out her infamous moves for fellow athletes at the 2024 Paris Olympic Games Closing Ceremony.

Aussie breaking athlete and viral internet sensation Rachael ‘Raygun’ Gunn has set tongues wagging again after she entertained fellow athletes at the 2024 Paris Olympic Games Closing Ceremony.

Gunn, a 36-year-old university lecturer, became the topic of widespread ridicule online when she lost all three pool matches against the USA, France and Lithuania in the sport’s opener at Paris’ Place de la Concorde on Friday.

The Aussie’s peculiar choice of dance moves was lashed online with a bizarre wriggle on the floor, clumsy roll and imitation of a kangaroo taking the spotlight.

Critics also lambasted her choice of the Australian tracksuit during her match ups, a stark contrast to her competitor’s hip streetwear styles.

But the breaker’s online torching did not appear to translate to the Closing Ceremony on Sunday where Gunn cracked out her infamous moves.

The Paris closing ceremony is unfolding with the Aussie athletes basking in the glory of Raygunâ€™s heroics in the lead in. Got to love the way they have got around herhttps://t.co/tNh4Kn3UsEpic.twitter.com/BiKNoVrXtu

Gunn was spotted alongside fellow Aussie Olympians shaking her stuff in the centre of cheering athletes.

Flaunting an array of moves, Gunn’s final Olympics performance was shared to Instagram by male diver Dom Bedggood who captioned his post: “Put it in the Louvre”.

She was even spotted on the shoulders of men’s rower Angus Widdicombe and marched through a tunnel of Aussie Olympians where she flaunted her kangaroo arms.

“The Queen has arrived for the closing ceremony,” one X, formerly Twitter, user captioned the video.

The Queen has arrived for the closing ceremony.pic.twitter.com/LYMJidfJS1

Her latest appearance quickly sparked fresh debate on social media, with some suggesting she performed better during the celebrations than at the Olympics.

"Why is her dancing here better than what she showcased in the actual competition?" one person wrote on X.

"My first thought was she danced better here than during the vid of her event," another said.

Some argued the effort was subpar, while others jumped to the defence of the Aussie "icon" who was having fun with her fellow Olympians.

"Like being at school getting around a kid that was no good at sport. Real participation award vibes," one wrote.

"Seen better at the office Christmas Party," a second said.

"Love how everyone is suddenly an expert on break dancing! Looks like the Australian team having fun to me. The very fact that everyone is talking about it (breaking) makes its inclusion at the Olympics a success," another argued.

"They all look like they are having s heaps of fun. Good on them," a four comment read.

Breaking judge Martin Gilian, who goes by MGbility, was earlier sympathetic to Gunn after she failed to score a single point in her three match ups.

“I feel personally very sorry,” he said at a press conference.

“The breaking and hip hop community definitely stands behind her. She was just trying to bring something new, something original and something that represents her country.”

Mr Gilian commended the Aussie breaker's originality at the games, despite this approach failing to score her a single point.

“Breaking is all about originality and bringing something new to the table and representing your country or region,” he said.

“This is exactly what Raygun was doing. She got inspired by her surroundings, which in this case, for example, was a kangaroo.”

Gunn even caught the support of Prime Minister Anthony Albanese who commended her for “having a go”.

“Good on her and a big shout-out to her. That is in the Australian tradition of people having a go. She’s had a go representing our country and that’s a good thing,” Mr Albanese said.

“The Olympics is about people participating in sport. That’s a good thing and Raygun had a crack.

“Whether (athletes have) won gold medals or just done their best, that is all we ask for. It’s the participating that is really important.”

Gunn’s support from her fellow Aussies and athletes came after a slew of hateful comments levelled at her online.

Many labelled her performance “disgraceful” and an “embarrassment” for Australians, as some likened it to the dancing of a young child.

Speaking to reporters after her Paris flop failed to maintain her status as a breaking great, Gunn admitted she was never going to win in 2024.

“All my moves are original,” she said.

“I was never going to beat these girls on what they do best, the dynamic and the power moves, so I wanted to move differently, be artistic and creative because how many chances do you get in a lifetime to do that on an international stage?

“I was always the underdog and wanted to make my mark in a different way.”

Breaking is not on the program for 2028 Los Angeles Olympic Games.

title: Aussies react as Rachael 'Raygun' pulls off 'better performance' at Closing Ceremony celebrations than at 2024 Paris Olympic Games | Sky News Australia
description: Aussie breaking athlete and viral internet sensation Rachael ‘Raygun’ Gunn has set tongues wagging again after she entertained fellow athletes at the 2024 Paris Olympic Games Closing Ceremony.
keywords: Rachael 'Raygun' Gunn,Rachael Gunn,Martin Gilian,Angus Widdicombe,MGbility,Raygun,Breakdancing,Sergey Nifontov,World DanceSport Federation,Dom Bedggood,Prime Minister Anthony Albanese,Breaking,Anthony Albanese,2024 Paris Olympic Games,Paris Olympics,Olympic Games
